1: The Angel of YHWH's appearance at Bokim ('weeping ones') is a covenant lawsuit (rîḇ) scene — the standard ANE and biblical form for a superior party confronting a covenant vassal with breach. The lawsuit form includes: divine self-identification ('I brought you up from Egypt'), recital of covenant benefits ('I brought you into the land I swore to your fathers'), statement of covenant demand ('you shall make no covenant with the inhabitants'), statement of covenant violation ('but you have not obeyed my voice'), and announcement of consequence ('I will not drive them out before you'). This is the same literary form as Exod 20:2 (preamble + historical prologue), Deut 31:15-22, and the classical prophets' lawsuits (Mic 6:1-8; Jer 2:1-13). The bokim (weeping) response is the covenant people's corporate lament at hearing the verdict.
8: Joshua's death and burial at Timnath-serah mark the transition from the Conquest era to the Judges era. The Deuteronomistic History (Deuteronomy through Kings) is structured around the deaths of its great leaders: Moses (Deut 34), Joshua (Josh 24:29-30; Judg 2:8-9), Samuel (1 Sam 25:1), and the death of the monarchy (2 Kgs 25). Joshua dies at 110 — the same age as Joseph (Gen 50:26), a literary parallelism that brackets the patriarchal-conquest era. The generation that knew the works of YHWH dying off is the theological explanation for the apostasy cycle: each new generation must encounter YHWH freshly, but the covenant transmission fails.
10: 'There arose another generation after them who did not know YHWH or the works that he had done for Israel.' The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) in covenant theology means more than intellectual knowledge; it means covenant intimacy and acknowledgment (cf. Amos 3:2 — 'Only you have I known among all the families of the earth'; Jer 22:16 — 'to know me is to do justice'). A generation that 'does not know YHWH' has broken the covenant relationship, not merely lost information. This failure of inter-generational covenant transmission — what Deut 6:4-9 and 11:18-21 were designed to prevent — is the proximate cause of the entire Judges cycle. The Passover meal (Exod 13:8-10), covenant renewal ceremonies (Josh 24), and the annual feast calendar all existed specifically to ensure each generation experienced and transmitted the foundational saving acts.
16: The Deuteronomic cycle is introduced in its full systematic form: Israel apostasizes → YHWH's anger → foreign oppressor → Israel cries out → YHWH raises a judge → deliverance → rest → judge dies → apostasy again. This framework was likely compiled in the 7th century BCE during Josiah's reform, using older source materials. The judge (šōpēṭ) is a charismatic military deliverer empowered by YHWH's Spirit — not a permanent magistrate but a crisis-response leader. The cycle's intensification across the book (each judge worse or the situation more complex than the last) serves the book's argument that Israel needs not just a better judge but a different kind of ruler: a king after YHWH's own heart.
23: 'YHWH left those nations, not driving them out quickly, and he did not give them into the hand of Joshua.' The theological explanation for why YHWH allowed the Canaanites to remain takes two forms in ch2-3: (1) to test Israel's covenant loyalty (2:22; 3:1,4) and (2) to teach warfare to those who had not known war (3:2). The 'testing' motif (nāsāh) appears throughout Deuteronomy (Deut 8:2 — YHWH tested Israel in the wilderness 'to know what was in your heart'); it frames covenant history as a proving ground. The Second Temple tradition (Josephus, Ant. V.2.3) saw the remaining Canaanites as part of a providential arrangement that preserved Israel's military readiness and dependence on YHWH.
2: The Angel's covenant indictment recites the specific obligations that Israel violated: 'make no covenant with the inhabitants of this land; tear down their altars.' These are the two key prohibitions of Exod 23:32-33; 34:12-16 and Deut 7:2-5. The breach was not ignorance but deliberate non-compliance with known covenant terms — making the judgment that follows covenantally inevitable rather than arbitrary.
3: The consequence YHWH announces — the remaining nations will be 'thorns in your sides' and 'their gods will be a snare to you' — is the negative fulfillment of the covenant warning in Exod 23:33; Num 33:55; Deut 7:16. The 'snare' (pah) metaphor is particularly apt: a trap is not immediately lethal but catches the unwary and holds them; the Canaanite gods would not immediately destroy Israel but would entangle Israel in progressive apostasy.
4: The people's weeping at Bochim upon hearing the Angel's words is the immediate, temporary response of covenant grief — the same pattern as Israel's weeping at Sinai after the golden calf incident. The narrator will note in vv11-19 that this remorse was not durable; the cycle of apostasy would begin again after Joshua's generation died. The weeping at Bochim is genuine grief, but grief without sustained structural change — a recurring diagnosis throughout Judges.
5: The place is named Bochim (bōkîm, 'weeping ones') after the event — an etiological naming in which a geographic site memorializes a spiritual moment. The practice of sacrificing at this site 'to YHWH' after the judgment-oracle is the appropriate covenantal response, but the sacrifice itself follows the weeping rather than preceding it, suggesting reparative rather than preventive worship. Bochim's location is uncertain; Josephus identified it with Bethel (Ant. 5.98), which would link it to the Bethel sanctuary of later Judges narratives.
6: The flashback to Joshua's dismissal of the people (parallel to Josh 24:28) repositions the reader chronologically: ch2:6 overlaps with Josh 24:28-31 to re-establish the timeline before narrating the post-Joshua degeneration. This literary technique — beginning a new section by backing up to a prior moment and running forward from it — is characteristic of the Deuteronomistic compositional method, which frames each historical period with a death of the previous leader and a transition to the new era.
7: The continuity of covenant fidelity is tied to living memory: 'the people served YHWH all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders who outlived Joshua, who had seen all the great work that YHWH had done for Israel.' When personal witnesses to the Exodus and conquest died, their testimony died with them. The problem the verse diagnoses is structural — Israel had not yet developed the liturgical and catechetical institutions needed to transmit covenant memory across generations without direct witnesses (cf. Deut 6:20-25 as the intended answer to this problem).
9: Joshua's burial at Timnath-heres ('portion of the sun') in the hill country of Ephraim — note the contrast with Josh 24:30 which names it Timnath-serah ('portion of abundance'). The two names may reflect different traditions or a deliberate reversal in one text. The burial site within his inheritance underscores the connection between Joshua and his tribal allotment — a leader who received the land he had fought for, dying in it and buried in it, which stands in contrast to Moses who was denied entry.
11: The programmatic verdict 'the people of Israel did what was evil in the sight of YHWH' (wayyaʿăśû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hāraʿ bĕʿênê Yhwh) is the Deuteronomistic History's standard evaluative formula, used throughout Judges and Kings to mark each cycle of apostasy. The phrase functions as a theological summary that simultaneously reports behavior and pronounces judgment from YHWH's perspective. Its repetition throughout Judges creates a rhythmic structure of failure against which the occasional deliverances stand out.
12: Forsaking YHWH 'the God of their fathers who had brought them out of Egypt' and serving the Baals (habbĕʿālîm) is the specific covenantal violation that triggers judgment. Baal (lord, owner) was the Canaanite storm deity associated with rainfall, fertility, and agricultural productivity — his worship was the perennial temptation in an agricultural society dependent on seasonal rain. The plural 'Baals' refers to the local manifestations of the storm deity worshipped at different hilltop shrines (bāmôt) throughout Canaan.
13: The pairing of Baal and Ashtaroth (hāʿaštārôt) represents the male and female divine pair of Canaanite religion — Baal the storm god and Astarte the goddess of love, war, and fertility. Ugaritic literature (discovered at Ras Shamra from 1929 onward) has provided extensive documentation of this divine pantheon. The plural 'Ashtaroth' (like 'Baals') indicates local goddess-shrines rather than a single entity. The prophets' consistent condemnation of Baal-Asherah/Astarte worship reflects the specific religious danger the Judges-era narrative is diagnosing at its root.
14: YHWH 'sold them into the hand of their enemies' (wayyimkōr ʾōtām bĕyad ʾôyĕbêhem) — the 'selling' (makar) language frames the covenant punishment as a commercial transaction: Israel has effectively been sold into slavery to foreign powers, the same condition from which YHWH had redeemed them from Egypt. The theological irony is pointed: by abandoning YHWH for Baals they return to the bondage-condition that defined their pre-YHWH existence. The plundering of Israel by enemies is the external expression of the internal disintegration caused by covenant betrayal.
15: YHWH's hand against Israel 'as YHWH had sworn' (kaʾăšer nidbber Yhwh) links the judgment explicitly to the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 — the judgment is not arbitrary anger but the execution of previously announced covenant consequences. The phrase 'they were in terrible distress' (wayyēṣer lāhem mĕʾōd) is the narrative signal that Israel has reached the nadir of the cycle, the moment of maximum pressure that precedes the cry for deliverance and the raising of a judge.
17: 'They did not listen to their judges, for they whored after other gods and bowed down to them' — the verb 'whored' (zānâ after) is the prophetic metaphor for covenant infidelity as marital betrayal. YHWH's relationship with Israel is figured as a marriage (cf. Hos 2:13-14; Jer 3:1-5; Ezek 16); idolatry is therefore adultery against the divine husband. The Judges narrator employs this metaphor structurally: the judges are not failed because YHWH's deliverance was insufficient but because Israel did not sustain covenant fidelity even during periods of successful leadership.
18: YHWH's compassion (niḥam, 'was moved to pity/relented') in response to Israel's groaning under oppression is the pivot of each judge cycle. The theological logic: even covenant-breaking Israel, when in sufficient suffering, elicits divine pity because of the covenant relationship — YHWH remembers not Israel's faithfulness (which has lapsed) but his own commitment to them. The empathy expressed — 'YHWH was moved to pity by their groaning because of those who afflicted and oppressed them' — characterizes YHWH as a deity who responds to suffering even in disobedient covenant partners.
19: 'But whenever the judge died, they turned back and were more corrupt than their fathers' — the progressive deterioration motif. Each generation's apostasy is worse than the previous: 'they did not drop any of their practices or their stubborn ways.' The Judges narrative has a downward trajectory throughout — the later judges (Jephthah, Samson) are morally more compromised than the earlier ones (Othniel, Gideon), reflecting the progressive corruption that v19 predicts. The book charts not just repeated cycles but a spiral of deepening covenant failure.
20: YHWH's anger and the covenant indictment — 'this people have transgressed my covenant that I commanded their fathers' — frames the theological diagnosis of the entire Judges period. The covenant is treated as a corporate, trans-generational obligation: the 'fathers' here are the Sinai generation; their descendants' apostasy violates a covenant they individually never ratified but are held responsible for as members of the covenant community. This collective covenant theology is the basis for prophetic calls to return to the ancestral covenant.
21: YHWH's decision not to drive out the remaining nations reverses the conquest promise — the blessing becomes withheld because the condition of faithfulness has not been met. This is a striking instance of conditional covenant logic: YHWH had promised to drive out the nations before Israel (Josh 3:10), but the condition was Israel's obedience. When the condition failed, the promise was suspended, and the remaining nations became instruments of discipline rather than targets of elimination.
22: The testing rationale — 'to test Israel, whether they will take care to walk in the way of YHWH as their fathers did' — gives a positive theological framing to what might appear as divine abandonment. The remaining Canaanites function as a proving ground: their presence is not YHWH's failure to complete the conquest but a deliberate educational instrument. This testing language connects to the Deuteronomic concept of the wilderness wandering as a test (Deut 8:2-3: 'he humbled you and tested you') — Israel's entire history is figured as a covenant examination.
